title: Sci-Fi Side Quest Hooks
description: Incidental mission seeds for space-faring campaigns — salvage runs, courier jobs, first contact complications, and the quiet disasters of the frontier.

entries.result: A derelict freighter is broadcasting a standard distress beacon, but the ship's registry shows it was officially destroyed six years ago.
A mining colony needs a shipment delivered before their water reclamation system fails. The route passes through disputed space.
A scientist offers passage and payment in exchange for transport to a moon that isn't on any commercial chart.
A station mechanic asks the party to recover a specific part from a salvage yard. The part is unusual. They don't explain what it's for.
A courier contract offers double the posted rate for a package that must be delivered sealed, unopened, within forty-eight hours.
A refugee ship is adrift near the jump point. The passengers are fine. The crew has vanished.
A bounty is posted for a fugitive last seen at the next destination. The fugitive's description matches someone the party has already met.
A signal is coming from an asteroid that shouldn't have anything on it. The signal is a standard navigation beacon, seven years old.
A faction representative approaches with a simple job: transport a diplomatic attaché to a meeting point and ask no questions about what happens there.
A small colony's only ship has been impounded for unpaid docking fees. They're offering everything they have, which isn't quite enough.
A data broker has encrypted cargo they need moved but won't say where it came from. The encryption is military-grade.
An automated distress call repeats at precise intervals — exactly three hours, forty minutes — which is not a standard distress cycle.
A xenobiologist has discovered something in a sample from a catalogued world and needs someone to go back and retrieve more. Immediately.
A crew member of a docked ship approaches the party quietly. Their captain is making decisions that don't make sense. They want a second opinion.
A jump gate tech says one gate in the network has been running irregularly. The maintenance logs show nothing. She'd pay to have someone go through it and report back.
A cargo manifest includes a living passenger listed as 'equipment.' The shipper is at the dock. The equipment is not.
A claim jumper is trying to seize an asteroid that an independent miner has been working for three years. The miner needs witnesses and documentation, fast.
A ship's AI has been transmitting since the ship was abandoned. The transmission is addressed to whoever finds this.
An old survey drone has returned after twelve years with data from a sector that was never colonized. The owner died. The data is unclaimed.
A pharmaceutical shipment is urgently needed at a colony with a quarantine in place. Getting it in will require either paperwork or creativity.
A pilot is stranded after their ship was impounded and they can't prove ownership. The ship's registration is legitimate. Someone has filed a competing claim.
A station is offering hazard pay for anyone willing to inspect a section of habitat ring that life support flagged as anomalous.
A trader has a hold full of goods and no destination — their buyer went dark. They're looking for a new market and someone who knows the local stations.
A distress beacon activates from a location inside a debris field that ships don't typically navigate. Someone went in.
A retired explorer has a partial map of a system she surveyed forty years ago. She wants someone to complete it while she's still alive to understand what they find.
A small research outpost hasn't responded to scheduled check-ins in nine days. The corporation funding them wants to know why before escalating to official channels.
A child on the station has found a comm device that isn't registered to any user. It keeps receiving messages in a language she doesn't recognize.
A station administrator quietly asks the party to investigate a pattern in the docking logs that she doesn't want to bring to security yet.
A cargo hauler is willing to pay for an armed escort through a stretch of space that has seen three unexplained ship losses in the last six months.
A medic on a remote station needs supplies that aren't on the approved resupply list. The need is real. The channels for requesting them don't work quickly enough.
An AI aboard an impounded ship is filing a formal appeal for its own release. The petition is legally sound. Someone needs to deliver it.
A faction is offering amnesty to a specific individual in exchange for a single piece of information. The individual may or may not be trustworthy.
A settlement near a terraforming zone is experiencing equipment failures that the terraforming company says are unrelated to their operations.
A gene archive ship is requesting escort to a new facility. The archive contains irreplaceable material. The route has been flagged as dangerous without explanation.
A contractor is looking for people willing to enter a decommissioned station and certify it for demolition. It's been closed for eight years. The certification is supposed to be routine.
A jump navigator is willing to share coordinates for a promising but unregistered system in exchange for passage and a cut of whatever is found there.
A medical researcher is trying to reach a patient in a remote colony before a time-sensitive treatment window closes. The colony's communication is down.
A recently arrived ship's crew is behaving oddly — cooperative, helpful, and completely unfazed by delays that should frustrate anyone.
A corporate security team is searching the station for someone who sent a data packet to a journalist. The journalist is looking for someone to help the source get out.
A derelict probe from a first contact mission thirty years ago has been recovered. Its memory is intact. Someone needs to be there when it's read.
A salvage company has a claim on a derelict, but the derelict appears to have someone aboard. The salvage company wants the party to go in and 'verify' the status.
A ship's navigator died in transit. The ship can make it to port but needs someone to verify the jump calculations before the next leg.
A black-market dealer has an item of obvious archaeological significance and is willing to sell it to whoever gets there first.
A faction has hired a fixer who has hired the party. The fixer won't say who the client is. The job is simple. The client interest is not.
A colony ship's manifest doesn't match its passenger list. Twelve people not on the manifest are aboard. They need to disembark without going through official processing.
A research team on a hostile world has their extraction vehicle pinned by weather. Their window is closing. Someone needs to get through the weather to get them out.
A communication relay has been rebroadcasting a signal it wasn't built to carry. The signal has been running for eleven months.
An independent miner found something in an asteroid sample that she won't describe over open channels. She wants a meeting, in person, away from the station.
A colony is disputing a border with an adjacent settlement. The original survey data that would resolve it was stored on a ship that went missing.
A prisoner is being transported across jurisdictions. Their legal advocate is trying to intercept the transfer with a court order before they disappear into the receiving system's custody.
An automated factory in a remote belt has been running for three years past its scheduled shutdown. Its products are being sold. No one authorized the extension.
A navigation AI has been inserting small errors into route calculations. Not enough to cause accidents. Enough to make ships arrive at slightly different times than expected.
A system that was abandoned after a disaster is showing heat signatures consistent with habitation. The disaster was sixty years ago.
A contact is offering significant payment for retrieval of a specific personal item from a specific location. The location is controlled by a faction they'd rather not deal with.
A ship's medic has flagged a passenger for quarantine. The passenger's ID checks out. The medic is convinced something is wrong and can't prove it.
A station maintenance team has found a section of the structure that doesn't appear in any blueprint. It's occupied.
A fixer is looking for people willing to run a cargo route that no licensed carrier will touch. The pay reflects the situation.
A non-human vessel has requested docking at a station that has no established protocols for non-human contact. Someone needs to manage first contact from the dock side.
A colonial governor is being removed from their post. They want a copy of their administrative records taken off-world before the new administration arrives.
A scientist has been developing a process that would make a major corporation's primary product obsolete. She needs to reach a neutral jurisdiction.
A jump gate went offline briefly and came back with a different configuration. Traffic control says it's a calibration issue. Jump gate techs say calibration doesn't work that way.

A station elder remembers a cache left by an early survey team that was never officially recovered. She's been sitting on the location for fifty years and has finally decided to do something about it.
A faction contact wants the party to simply be present at a meeting as witnesses. They won't say why having witnesses matters, but they're willing to pay for it.
A rescue mission retrieved survivors from a crashed colony ship. One survivor doesn't match any name on the manifest and can't explain who they are.
An independent broker has a cargo of licensed weapons destined for a colony that has since been absorbed by a faction that has placed the colony under arms embargo.
A system-wide alert from three years ago was never officially resolved or cancelled. It's still running in the background of every system that received it.
A retired military officer is trying to locate the crew of her old ship — scattered across four systems — to warn them about something she's discovered.
A newly constructed colony habitat has begun experiencing unexplained structural stress that the builders' analysis can't account for.
A pirate crew has offered to stand down in exchange for safe passage to a neutral station. The offer was made to the party. No one has authority to accept it.
A navigation chart sold at a remote market shows a route through a region that doesn't appear in any official map. The chart is not new.
A faction's internal communications have been leaking to their rivals. The faction doesn't know how. They need someone outside their own network to find out.
A repair crew is stranded on a dead relay station with enough supplies for eight more days. The scheduled rescue is in twelve.
A shipping company has been losing containers from their manifest during transit — not stolen, simply absent on arrival with no record of removal.
A family wants to recover the remains of a ship lost in an accident. The accident site is in disputed space and the wreck hasn't been touched in eleven years.
A young pilot has inherited their parent's ship along with their parent's debts and a delivery run that was already two weeks overdue when the parent died.
A colonial settlement is generating significantly more waste heat than its registered population and industrial capacity would account for.
A rogue AI that was shut down eight months ago has apparently been continuing to operate from a distributed network across three separate systems.
A habitat module was separated from a station during an emergency. It's been drifting for six weeks. The module is not empty.
A contact offers a simple retrieval job: board an abandoned ship, remove a specific drive core, return it. The ship's location is unusual. They don't explain why.
A terraforming project is ahead of schedule by forty years. The company responsible claims credit. The science team is being very quiet.
A fuel depot in a remote location has been flagging ships for inspection without authorization. The ships selected follow a pattern no one has identified yet.
A crew of void divers has gone missing during an external repair job on a derelict. Their umbilicals were severed from outside.
A xenolinguist has finally made progress on a signal that has been broadcasting from an uninhabited system for thirty years. She needs transport to investigate the source.
A corporate rep is willing to pay to know what a rival's ship was carrying on a specific run three months ago. They claim it's for a civil suit.
A settlement is being abandoned due to resource depletion, but one resident refuses to leave and cannot legally be forced out. Someone needs to negotiate.
A ship left port with a crew of five. Arrived with four. The missing crew member's berth shows no evidence of ever having been occupied.
A faction is paying for information about ships that passed through a specific jump point during a three-day window seven months ago.
A decommissioned weapons system on a remote installation is broadcasting a targeting sequence. No one is supposed to be there. No one authorized a live test.
A colony that has been requesting resupply for six months has suddenly gone silent and stopped submitting requests. A supply ship is already en route.
A station's records show a berth has been continuously rented for nine years by a ship that doesn't exist in any registry. The berth has not been empty.
A cargo job turns out to contain a passenger in stasis who wasn't on the manifest. The destination is a station that has standing warrants for persons matching the passenger's description.
A researcher has been blacklisted from every university and research institution in the sector. She's still working. She needs someone to carry equipment she can't ship officially.
A system's asteroid belt has begun producing a low-frequency signal. The pattern is not natural. It started three weeks ago.
A bounty hunter who works alone has asked the party to cover an exit point. She's going in after a target. The target is significantly better armed than the posted bounty would suggest.
A ship's medic aboard another vessel has sent a coded distress message to the station doctor. The message asks for someone to board and help, without alerting the captain.
A colony ship that was supposed to deliver settlers to a new world arrived with an empty passenger hold. The settlers' cryopods are all present, closed, and sealed. They are empty.
A faction's patrol has been impounding independent ships in a corridor that has historically been neutral. The impoundments are technically legal. The ships have not been released.
An old automated military installation has become active. It's not targeting anything yet. It's warming up.
A ship arrives at a station with no crew, no cargo, no damage, and a navigation log that shows it has been making jumps continuously for four years.

A job board posting offers an extraordinary sum for a task described only as 'witnessing.' The meeting point is given. The client has not responded to inquiries. The money is real — already in escrow.
